{{Short description|Men's association football team}}
{{About|the men's team|the women's team|Honduras women's national football team}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=June 2026}}

| First game            = {{fb|GUA|1871}} 9–0 {{fb-rt|HON|1898}}<br>([[Guatemala City]], Guatemala; 14 September 1921)
| Largest win           = {{fb|HON|1898}} 10–0 {{fb-rt|NIC|1908}}<br>([[San José, Costa Rica|San José]], Costa Rica; 13 March 1946)
| Largest loss          = {{fb|GUA|1871}} 9–0 {{fb-rt|HON|1898}}<br>([[Guatemala City]], Guatemala; 14 September 1921)
| World cup apps        = 3
| World cup first       = 1982
| World cup best        = Group stage ([[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]], [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010]], [[2014 FIFA World Cup|2014]])
| Regional name         = [[CONCACAF Championship]] / [[CONCACAF Gold Cup|Gold Cup]]
| Regional cup apps     = 23<!--as of 2025-->
| Regional cup first    = [[1963 CONCACAF Championship|1963]]
| Regional cup best     = '''Champions''' ([[1981 CONCACAF Championship|1981]])
| 2ndRegional name      = [[CONCACAF Nations League]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 4<!--as of 2025-->
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[2019–20 CONCACAF Nations League|2019–20]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  = Third place ([[2021 CONCACAF Nations League Finals|2021]])
| 3rdRegional name      = [[Copa América]]
| 3rdRegional cup apps  = 1
| 3rdRegional cup first = [[2001 Copa América|2001]]
| 3rdRegional cup best  = Third place ([[2001 Copa América|2001]])
| 4thRegional name      = [[CCCF Championship]]
| 4thRegional cup apps  = 6
| 4thRegional cup first = [[1946 CCCF Championship|1946]]
| 4thRegional cup best  = Runners-up ([[1953 CCCF Championship|1953]])
|World cup last=2014}}

The '''Honduras national football team''' ({{langx|es|Selección de fútbol de Honduras}}) represents [[Honduras]] in men's international [[Association football|football]], which is governed by the [[National Autonomous Federation of Football of Honduras|Federación de Fútbol de Honduras]] ({{langx|en|Federation of Football of Honduras}}) founded in 1935.
It has been an affiliate member of [[FIFA]] since 1951 and a founding affiliate member of [[CONCACAF]] since 1961. Regionally, it is an affiliate member of [[Central American Football Union|UNCAF]] in the Central American Zone.
From 1938 to 1961, it was a member of [[Confederación Centroamericana y del Caribe de Fútbol|CCCF]], the former governing body of football in Central America and Caribbean and a predecessor confederation of CONCACAF, and also a member of [[Panamerican Championship#Panamerican Football Confederation|PFC]], the unified confederation of the Americas, from 1946 to 1961.

Honduras has qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] three times ([[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]], [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010]] and [[2014 FIFA World Cup|2014]]).

Honduras has participated twenty-three times in CONCACAF's premier continental competition, it is one of three Central American teams to have won the competition, winning the [[CONCACAF Championship]] in [[1981 CONCACAF Championship|1981]] as hosts. The team's best performance under the [[CONCACAF Gold Cup]] format was finishing as runners-up in [[1991 Gold Cup|1991]]. It has participated four times in League A of the [[CONCACAF Nations League]], finishing in third place in the [[2021 CONCACAF Nations League Finals|2021 Finals]].
It has also participated once in the [[Copa América]], finishing in third place in [[2001 Copa América|2001]]. 

Regionally, the team won 4 [[Copa Centroamericana]] titles and also finished as runners-up in the [[CCCF Championship]] in [[1953 CCCF Championship|1953]].

==History==
[[File:Honduras en la Copa CCCF 1946, Estadio, 1946-03-23 (149).jpg|thumb|The Honduras national football team in 1946]]

The national team made its debut in the [[Independence Centenary Games]] held in [[Guatemala City]] in September 1921, losing 9–0 to [[Guatemala national football team|Guatemala]].<ref>{{cite web|last=Courtney|first=Barrie|title=Honduras International Soccer Matches Since 1920|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesh/hond-intres.html|date=13 November 2006|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=2 November 2010|archive-date=21 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221021004110/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesh/hond-intres.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The first [[association football|football]] match. 

During their first appearance at the [[Central American and Caribbean Games]] in [[1930 Central American and Caribbean Games|1930]], Honduras posted a record of two wins and three losses. Their only wins came against [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] (5–1) and [[El Salvador national football team|El Salvador]] (4–1), while they lost two games to [[Cuba national football team|Cuba]] and [[Costa Rica national football team|Costa Rica]].

===1970 World Cup and the Football War===
Prior to the qualification stages leading up to the [[1970 FIFA World Cup|1970 World Cup]] in Mexico, Honduras and El Salvador found themselves in what was called the [[Football War]]. This nickname was given to the situation after a play-off game was played between the two countries to decide which would qualify for the Finals. This political crisis eventually turned into a war that lasted approximately 100 hours.

Honduras had begun qualifying by defeating Costa Rica and Jamaica. Against Jamaica, they easily won both games, 5–1 on aggregate. They beat Costa Rica 1–0 in [[Tegucigalpa]] and drew 1–1 away. This set up a final match between Honduras and  El Salvador, who had eliminated Guyana and the Netherlands Antilles.

In the first game against El Salvador, Honduras won 1–0 in Tegucigalpa on 8 June 1969. Honduras were coached by [[Carlos Padilla Velásquez]] and the only goal of the game was scored by Leonard Welch. Honduras lost the second game 3–0 in [[San Salvador]], and a play-off was required in the [[Estadio Azteca|Azteca Stadium]] in Mexico City on 27 June. El Salvador won 3–2 to qualify and eliminate Honduras from the qualifications.

===1982 World Cup===
{{main|1982 FIFA World Cup}}

[[File:Honduras football crest 1976.svg|thumb|Crest in 1976.]]
Honduras won the [[1981 CONCACAF Championship]] and qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]] for the first time in [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]]. Despite drawing against the hosts [[Spain national football team|Spain]] and [[Northern Ireland national football team|Northern Ireland]], both 1–1, they were eliminated in the first round after losing their last match to [[Yugoslavia national football team|Yugoslavia]] 1–0.
[[File:Selecció de futbol d'Hondures. Copa del Món de Futbol de 1982. (Alginet, País Valencià).jpg|thumb|The Honduran team at the 1982 FIFA World Cup]]
{{:1982 FIFA World Cup Group 5|transcludesection=standings|showteam=HON}}
Honduras finished second in the [[1985 CONCACAF Championship]], losing their final match 2–1 against [[Canada men's national soccer team|Canada]], who went on to qualify for the [[1986 FIFA World Cup|1986 World Cup]]. Their next major accomplishment was being runners-up at the [[1991 CONCACAF Gold Cup]], losing against the host nation, the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]].

For the [[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998 World Cup]], [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] and [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]] eliminated Honduras at the [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONCACAF)#Group 3 2|third round]] stage. Despite Honduras's overwhelming 11–3 victory against [[Saint Vincent and the Grenadines national football team|Saint Vincent & the Grenadines]], Jamaica defeated Mexico at [[Independence Park (Jamaica)|Independence Park, Kingston]], allowing the Reggae Boys to advance to the next round.

===2001 Copa América===
{{main|2001 Copa América}}

Since 1993, [[CONMEBOL]] has invited teams from other confederations to participate in their confederation championship, the [[Copa América]]. Honduras took part as one of the last-minute teams added for the 2001 tournament, as [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]] dropped out one day before the start. The team arrived only a few hours before the tournament's first game and with barely enough players. Despite the odds, Honduras progressed into the quarter-finals, where they defeated [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]] 2–0. In the semi-finals, [[Colombia national football team|Colombia]] knocked out Honduras 2–0.

Honduras advanced to the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – CONCACAF final round|final round]] in the qualifying competition for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup]], but again failed to qualify after losing at home to [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad & Tobago]], and away against Mexico in their final two matches. The match against Trinidad, and Tobago saw Honduras hit the goal post eight times.{{citation needed|date=March 2020}}

===2010 World Cup===
{{main|2010 FIFA World Cup}}

[[File:FIFA World Cup 2010 Spain Honduras.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Walter Martínez (footballer, born 1982)|Walter Martinez]] and [[Iker Casillas]] of [[Spain national football team|Spain]] at the 2010 FIFA World Cup]]
On 14 October 2009, Honduras qualified for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010 World Cup]] after a 1–0 win against [[El Salvador national football team|El Salvador]] gave them the third automatic qualifying spot from the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification - CONCACAF fourth round|Fourth round of CONCACAF Qualifying]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://soccernet.espn.go.com/report?id=279542&cc=5739|title=Pavon puts visitors through|date=2009-10-14|work=ESPN|access-date=2009-10-15|archive-date=19 October 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019204450/http://soccernet.espn.go.com/report?id=279542&cc=5739|url-status=dead}}</ref>
{{main|2010 FIFA World Cup Group H}}

Honduras faced [[Chile national football team|Chile]], [[Spain national football team|Spain]], and [[Switzerland national football team|Switzerland]] in their first-round group.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/internationals/8393328.stm | work=BBC News | title=England enjoy kind World Cup draw | date=4 December 2009 | access-date=4 May 2010 | archive-date=3 April 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230403181851/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/internationals/8393328.stm | url-status=live }}</ref> In their first match they lost to Chile 1–0, to a goal from [[Jean Beausejour]]. They then lost 2–0 to Spain, with both goals scored by [[David Villa]]. In their last match they drew 0–0 against Switzerland and were eliminated in last place in the group.
{{:2010 FIFA World Cup Group H|transcludesection=standings|showteam=HON}}

===2014 World Cup===
{{main|2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONCACAF)}}

[[File:Canada vs. Honduras 2014 FIFA WCQ in Toronto (photo by Djuradj Vujcic).jpg|right|thumb|Players lining up during the [[National Anthem of Honduras|national anthem]] prior to the qualifying match against [[Canada men's national soccer team|Canada]] on June 12, 2012, at [[BMO Field]]]]

In the qualifying competition for the 2014 World Cup, Honduras were given a bye to the third round because of their third-place position among CONCACAF teams in the March 2011 [[FIFA World Rankings]]. They qualified for the final round by finishing first in their group, which included [[Panama national football team|Panama]], Canada and [[Cuba national football team|Cuba]]. After beginning with a home defeat against Panama, Honduras recovered and beat Canada 8–1 in their final match, allowing them to win the group ahead of Panama.

In the final round of qualifying, the [[Hexagonal (CONCACAF)|Hexagonal]], six teams faced each other in a home-and-away format. In their first two games, Honduras defeated the United States 2–1 and came back from a two-goal deficit to draw 2–2 with [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]]. They lost three of their next four matches before traveling to [[Mexico City]] to face Mexico in the [[Estadio Azteca|Azteca]]. Honduras again trailed but scored twice in the second half for a stunning [[Aztecazo#Honduran Aztecazo|2–1 win]]. They returned to Tegucigalpa, where they drew 2–2 against Panama, who escaped defeat with a last-minute goal by [[Roberto Chen]]. In the final two games, Honduras beat Costa Rica 1–0 at home and qualified with a 2–2 draw against Jamaica in [[Kingston, Jamaica|Kingston]].
{{:2014 FIFA World Cup qualification –  CONCACAF fourth round|showteam=HON}}
{{main|2014 FIFA World Cup Group E}}

In the [[2014 FIFA World Cup|tournament]] in Brazil, Honduras again finished bottom of their first-round group, after 3–0 defeats against [[France national football team|France]] and Switzerland, and a 2–1 defeat to [[Ecuador national football team|Ecuador]]. The [[2014 FIFA World Cup Group E#France vs Honduras|match against France]] featured the first use of [[goal-line technology]] to award a goal at the World Cup: an own-goal by Honduras' goalkeeper, [[Noel Valladares]]. The goal scored by [[Carlo Costly]] against Ecuador was Honduras' first goal in the World Cup in 32 years.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.foxnews.com/sports/honduras-scores-1st-world-cup-goal-in-32-years-level-1-1-with-ecuador-at-halftime-in-group-e |title=Honduras scores 1st World Cup goal in 32 years, level 1-1 with Ecuador at halftime in Group E |date=20 June 2014 |publisher=[[Fox News]] |access-date=22 March 2026}}</ref>
{{:2014 FIFA World Cup Group E|transcludesection=standings|showteam=HON}}

===Decline===

{{:2018 FIFA World Cup qualification –  CONCACAF fifth round|showteam=HON}}
In 2017, Honduras failed to qualify for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup|2018 World Cup]]. In the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – CONCACAF fifth round|Hexagonal stage]], they had mixed run of results which included draws against Costa Rica and Panama. On the final matchday, however, Honduras defeated Mexico 3–2 in San Pedro Sula, and coupled with a 2–1 Panamanian victory against Costa Rica and a [[Trinidad and Tobago v United States (2018 FIFA World Cup qualification)|United States loss to Trinidad and Tobago]], Honduras finished in the fourth position to advance to a [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONCACAF–AFC play-off)|play-off]] against [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]]. Following  a 0–0 draw at home, Honduras were eliminated in the second leg in [[Sydney]] with a 3–1 loss. This defeat resulted in the departure of head coach [[Jorge Luis Pinto]], and would be the final involvement of national team veterans [[Mario Martínez (footballer, born 1989)|Mario Martínez]], [[Johnny Palacios]], [[Donis Escober]], and Carlo Costly.

Heading into the [[2019 CONCACAF Gold Cup]], Uruguayan manager [[Fabián Coito]] was appointed as the new head coach of Honduras. Drawn in [[2019 CONCACAF Gold Cup Group C|Group C]] with expectations to advance to the next round, Honduras finished last in the group, following an opening loss to [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] and an upset defeat to [[Curaçao national football team|Curaçao]]. Despite a 4–0 win over El Salvador in their final group stage match, Honduras finished last in the group. Heading into the tournament with a newer pool of players, this was the final involvement for veteran full-back duo [[Emilio Izaguirre]] and [[Brayan Beckeles]].

In the summer of 2021, Honduras had a strong showing in the inaugural [[2021 CONCACAF Nations League Finals|CONCACAF Nations League Finals]], defeating rivals Costa Rica in a [[Penalty shoot-out (association football)|penalty-shootout]] in the third place play-off, having been eliminated by the United States 1–0 in the semi-finals. In the [[2021 CONCACAF Gold Cup]], Honduras would perform better in the group stage largely in part to the offensive contributions of forward duo [[Alberth Elis]] and [[Romell Quioto]], but after the pair both suffered injuries in the group stage, Honduras was eliminated by Mexico 3–0 in the quarter-finals.

Heading into [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – CONCACAF third round|2022 FIFA World Cup qualification]] in the new "Octagonal" format, Honduras was expected to contend in the qualification spots, however they did considerably worse, as for the first time ever in a World Cup qualification cycle, the team had failed to register a win, with just four draws and ten losses. Despite an initial draw against the eventual first-placed nation Canada, Honduras spiraled in form after suffering a 4–1 loss to the United States in San Pedro Sula, despite leading at half-time. After a string of poor results, head coach Coito was sacked and replaced by [[Hernán Darío Gómez]]. Despite the managerial change, the poor run of form would continue as Gómez would experiment with different players, leading to the inability to properly replace past veterans and develop a cohesive squad.
{{:2022 FIFA World Cup qualification –  CONCACAF third round|showteam=HON}}

The team's poor showing in qualifying highlighted the main issues within Honduran football, with some pundits bringing attention to said issues, which included poor leadership and direction from [[National Autonomous Federation of Football of Honduras|FENAFUTH]], poor technical staff, and a negligence from the federation towards player infrastructure. The lack of support for proper youth leagues and player development, as well as outdated and limited training facilities were key points in regards to the poor player infrastructure, which also contributed to the poor quality of the [[Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional de Honduras|national league]], leading to the stagnation in Honduran football.<ref>{{Cite web |last=López|first=Elmer|title=La Selección Nacional de Honduras y las reflexiones de una eliminatoria perdida |trans-title=The Honduran National Team and reflections on a lost qualifying campaign |url=https://www.diez.hn/blogs/seleccion-nacional-honduras-reflexiones-de-una-eliminatoria-perdida-mundial-qatar-DKDZ1502809|date=17 November 2021|access-date=7 July 2025|website=[[La Prensa (Honduras)|Diario Diez]] |language=es}}</ref> After FENAFUTH released a statement in regards to the team's failure, former player and top all-time goalscorer for the national team [[Carlos Pavón]] criticized the federation, proclaiming, "Are you serious? How easy it is to be a leader in Honduras, to justify an eight-year failure in a communiqué. The ideal thing would be for the famous committee to show their faces at a press conference and explain what they wrote, wouldn't they?."<ref>{{Cite web |title=Honduras se disculpa por el último lugar del Octagonal Final y Carlos Pavón explota |trans-title=Honduras apologizes for finishing last in the Octagonal and Carlos Pavón explodes |url=https://www.tudn.com/futbol/concacaf-mundial-eliminatorias/honduras-disculpa-aficion-ultimo-lugar-octagonal-final-mundial-qatar-2022 |access-date=7 July 2025 |website=[[TUDN (brand)|TUDN]] |date=31 March 2022 }}</ref>

At the [[2023 CONCACAF Gold Cup]], Honduras made a group stage exit, missing out on qualification into the next round due to a lesser goal difference against invited guests [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Honduras derrota a Haití con polémica, pero queda eliminada de Copa Oro |trans-title=Honduras defeats Haiti amid controversy, but is eliminated from the Gold Cup |url=https://www.tudn.com/futbol/copa-oro/honduras-derrota-haiti-con-polemica-pero-queda-eliminada-cuartos-de-final-copa-oro |access-date=18 February 2026 |website=TUDN |date=2 July 2023}}</ref>

In late 2023, Honduras began its journey in the [[2023–24 CONCACAF Nations League|2023–24 edition]] of the CONCACAF Nations League. The team was placed in Group B of [[2023–24 CONCACAF Nations League A|League A]] and finished in second, qualifying alongside Jamaica to the quarter-finals, where a spot at the [[2024 Copa América]] was at stake against Mexico. In a two-legged affair, Honduras pulled off an upset in the first leg, defeating Mexico 2–0 in Tegucigalpa with goals from [[Anthony Lozano]] and [[Bryan Róchez]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.laprensa.hn/fotogalerias/deportes/honduras-mexico-reacciones-prensa-hondurena-nations-league-copa-america-GH16337247|title=Prensa se rinde ante Honduras y desata locura tras triunfo ante México|trans-title=Press surrenders to Honduras and unleashes madness after victory over Mexico|newspaper=La Prensa|language=es|date=17 November 2023|access-date=22 February 2026}}</ref> In the return leg in [[Mexico City]], ''El Tri'' leveled the aggregate score with a 2–0 win and took the tie to a penalty-shootout, where Mexico were victorious 4–2 and secured a spot in the Copa América. The match became infamous, especially in Honduras, due to referring decisions made by Salvadoran referee [[Iván Barton]]. The most notable instances that were highlighted was the equalizing goal scored by [[Edson Álvarez]], which came two minutes after the 9 minutes of added time had already exceeded, and the second instance was during the penalty-shootout when [[César Huerta]] was allowed to retake his penalty three times because the Honduran goalkeeper [[Edrick Menjívar]] had been off his line the first two times.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://espndeportes.espn.com/futbol/mexico/nota/_/id/12901465/mexico-honduras-prensa-explota-por-triunfo-estadio-azteca-robo|title=Prensa hondureña explota por triunfo de México: Robo en el Azteca|trans-title=Honduran press explodes over Mexico's victory: Theft at the Azteca|website=ESPN Deportes|language=es|date=21 November 2023|access-date=22 February 2026}}</ref> Honduras later advanced to the [[2024 Copa América qualifying play-offs|Copa América play-in]] against Costa Rica for a last opportunity to qualify, but were defeated 3–1.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Costa Rica recover from early deficit to down Honduras|url=https://www.concacaf.com/nations-league/news/costa-rica-recover-from-early-deficit-to-down-honduras/|date=24 March 2024|access-date=22 March 2026|publisher=[[CONCACAF]]}}</ref>

In late 2024, during the [[2024–25 CONCACAF Nations League|2024–25 edition]] of the CONCACAF Nations League, Honduras encountered Mexico once again in the quarter-finals, with a spot in the [[2025 CONCACAF Nations League Finals|finals]] and a place in the [[2025 CONCACAF Gold Cup]] on the line. In [[San Pedro Sula]], Honduras would once again emerge victorious in the first leg, 2–0, thanks to a brace from [[Luis Palma]]. Mexico overturned the deficit with a 4–0 win in the second leg, qualifying to the Finals and sending Honduras to a [[2025 CONCACAF Gold Cup qualification|Gold Cup qualification]] tie against [[Bermuda national football team|Bermuda]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/report/_/gameId/721463 |title=Palma leads Honduras to Nations League victory over Mexico |last= Hernandez |first=Cesar |date=15 November 2024 |publisher=[[ESPN]] |access-date=22 March 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.si.com/soccer/el-tri-completes-the-comeback-takeaways-from-mexico-s-victory-over-honduras|title=El Tri Completes the Comeback: Takeaways From Mexico's Victory Over Honduras|last= Casillas |first=Roberto |website=Sports Illustrated|language=en|date=20 November 2024|access-date=22 March 2026}}</ref>

After defeating Bermuda 7–3 on aggregate over two legs, Honduras opened their 2025 Gold Cup campaign with a 6–0 loss to Canada. Despite the initial setback, ''La H'' recorded wins against El Salvador and Curaçao, finishing second in their group. In what was seen a surprise and upset by some, Honduras would prevail over Panama in the quarter-finals. After finishing 1–1 in regulatory time, Honduras advanced 5–4 on penalties.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/soccer/2025/06/28/honduras-panama-penalties-score-gold-cup-highlights/84404067007/|title=Honduras shocks Panama in penalties for trip to Gold Cup semifinals. How it happened|last= Williams |first=James |website=[[USA Today]]|language=en|date=28 June 2025|access-date=22 March 2026}}</ref> ''Los Catrachos'' would then face Mexico in the semifinals, where they lost 1–0.

In late 2025, Honduras entered the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – CONCACAF third round|third round of World Cup qualifying]]. With the expansion to a 48-team World Cup and the automatic qualification of the hosts United States, Mexico, and Canada, this cycle was seen as an opportunity to for Honduras and other Central American nations to carve an easy path to the [[2026 FIFA World Cup]], but several factors turned this cycle into a drastic failure.<ref>{{cite news |last=Arnold |first=Jon |date=21 November 2025 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/6824174/2025/11/21/concacaf-world-cup-qualifying-usmnt-haiti-curacao/ |title=Concacaf World Cup qualifying erupted into wondrous chaos minus USMNT, Mexico, Canada |newspaper=[[The Athletic]] |access-date=5 April 2026}} {{subscription required|s}}</ref> Drawn into Group C with Costa Rica, [[Haiti national football team|Haiti]], and [[Nicaragua national football team|Nicaragua]], Honduras recorded 8 points after four fixtures with two games remaining, and ''Los Catrachos'' needed just one win to secure direct qualification to the World Cup, but in a shock upset, Honduras lost to Nicaragua 2–0 in [[Managua]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nicaragua defeats Honduras and keeps Group C on edge|url=https://www.concacaf.com/en/world-cup-qualifying-men/news/nicaragua-defeats-honduras-and-keeps-group-c-on-edge/|date=14 November 2025|access-date=5 April 2026|publisher=[[CONCACAF]]}}</ref> Despite the setback, Honduras still sat in the first position of Group C, but still needed to secure a win against Costa Rica in [[San José, Costa Rica|San José]]. In a must-win for both nations, the final score on matchday six was 0–0, effectively eliminating both Honduras and Costa Rica from World Cup contention.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Costa Rica and Honduras end World Cup dreams in scoreless draw|url=https://www.concacaf.com/en/world-cup-qualifying-men/news/costa-rica-and-honduras-end-world-cup-dreams-in-scoreless-draw/#:~:text=in%20scoreless%20draw-,Costa%20Rica%20and%20Honduras%20end%20World%20Cup%20dreams%20in%20scoreless,1%2D4%2D1%20record.|date=19 November 2025|access-date=9 April 2026|publisher=[[CONCACAF]]}}</ref> Both teams required a win to control their own destiny, in Honduras' case, they needed at least a 1–0 win to still advance automatically. While Honduras finished second in their group and tied with [[Suriname national football team|Suriname]] for the final [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (inter-confederation play-offs)|inter-confederation playoff spot]], they were eliminated because Suriname scored more goals (9 vs. 5) throughout the qualifying round. Honduras drew heavy criticism from supporters and analysts alike for what was seen as "tactical suicide" because they actively wasted time during the final minutes of their 0–0 draw with Costa Rica, seemingly unaware that a draw would eliminate them both. Instead of pushing for a winning goal which they needed to stay ahead of Suriname in the tiebreaker, Honduran players continued to run down the clock as if the draw were a favorable result.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Honduras: Tercer fracaso mundialista y 12 años hundidos con los mismos de la FFH y Comisión de Selecciones |url=https://www.diez.hn/laseleccion/honduras-tercer-fracaso-mundialista-hundidos-mismos-ffh-comision-selecciones-eliminatoria-HN28253884|date=18 November 2025|access-date=9 April 2026|website=[[La Prensa (Honduras)|Diario Diez]] |language=es}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=El fracaso de Honduras ya cobró primera “víctima” |url=https://www.telemundo.com/deportes/copa-mundial-de-la-fifa-2026/el-fracaso-de-honduras-ya-cobro-primera-victima-rcna244817|date=19 November 2025|access-date=9 April 2026|website=[[Telemundo]] |language=es}}</ref>

==Home stadium==

Honduras plays the majority of its home games at [[Estadio Nacional Chelato Uclés]] in [[Tegucigalpa]].

The national team formerly played at [[Estadio Olímpico Metropolitano]] in [[San Pedro Sula]], which stopped being the home stadium due to declining facilities.  [[Estadio Francisco Morazán]] also occasionally hosts home games.{{citation needed|date=July 2020}}

[[Estadio Nilmo Edwards]] in [[La Ceiba]] has also hosted friendly exhibition matches since 2007.{{citation needed|date=July 2020}}

===Coaching history===
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|HON|}} [[Aristides Raudales |  Aristides Raudales]] (1921)
*{{flagicon|HON|}} [[Octavio Arriola | Octavio Arriola]] (1930)
*{{flagicon|HON|}} [[ Jacobo de Fuenquinos |  Jacobo de Fuenquinos]] (1935)
*{{flagicon|HUN|}} [[István Kovács(Honduras football coach)| István Kovács]] (1946)
*{{flagicon|HON|}} [[Lurio Martinez(Honduras football coach)| Lurio Martinez]] (1953)
*{{flagicon|BRA|}} [[Otto Bumbel| Otto Bumbel]] (1955–1956)
*{{flagicon|HON|}} [[Mario Griffin Cubas| Mario Grifffin Cubas]] (1961)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Carlos Padilla (Honduras football coach)|Carlos Padilla]] (1960–1962)
*{{flagicon|BRA|1960}} [[Elsy Núñez Gonzales|Elsy Núñez]] (1962–1966)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Marinho Rodríguez]] (1966–1967)
*{{flagicon|CHI}} [[Sergio Lecea]] (1967–1968)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Carlos Padilla (Honduras football coach)|Carlos Padilla]] (1968–1973)
*{{flagicon|GER}} [[Peter Lange (football manager)|Peter Lange]] (1974–1976)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[José de la Paz Herrera|José Herrera]] (1980–1986)
*{{flagicon|NED}} [[Ger Blok]] (1987–1988)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[José de la Paz Herrera|José Herrera]] (1988)
*{{flagicon|BRA|1968}} [[Flavio Ortega]] (1991–1992)
*{{flagicon|URU}} [[Estanislao Malinowski]] (1992–1993)
*{{flagicon|URU}} [[Julio González Montemurro|Julio González]] (1993)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Carlos Cruz Carranza|Carlos Cruz]] (1995)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[Ernesto Rosa Guedes|Ernesto Rosa]] (1996)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Ramón Maradiaga]] (1996)
*{{flagicon|PER}} [[Miguel Company]] (1997–1998)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Ramón Maradiaga]] (1998–2002)
*{{flagicon|URU|1942}} [[Ernesto Luzardo]] (2002)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Edwin Pavón]] (2003)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[José de la Paz Herrera|José Herrera]] (2003)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[René Simões]] (2003)
*{{flagicon|SCG}} [[Bora Milutinović]] (2003–2004)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[José de la Paz Herrera|José Herrera]] (2005)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Raúl Martínez Sambulá|Raúl Martínez]] (2006)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[Flavio Ortega]] (2006–2007)
*{{flagicon|COL}} [[Reinaldo Rueda]] (2007–2010)
*{{flagicon|MEX}} [[Juan de Dios Castillo|Juan Castillo]] (2010–2011)
*{{flagicon|COL}} [[Luis Fernando Suárez|Luis Suárez]] (2011–2014)
*{{flagicon|CRC}} [[Hernán Medford]] (2014)
*{{flagicon|COL}} [[Jorge Luis Pinto|Jorge Pinto]] (2014–2017)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} [[Carlos Tábora]] (2018)
*{{flagicon|HON|1949}} Jorge Jimenez (2018)
*{{flagicon|URU}} [[Fabián Coito]] (2019–2021)
*{{flagicon|COL}} [[Hernán Darío Gómez]] (2021–2022)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Diego Vásquez]] (2022–2023)
*{{flagicon|COL}} [[Reinaldo Rueda]] (2023–2025)
* {{flagicon|ESP}} [[José Francisco Molina|Francisco Molina]] (2026–present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following 24 players were called up for the [[Exhibition game|friendly]] match against [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]] on 6 June 2026.<ref>Oficial: Convocatoria de la Selección de Honduras ante Argentina de Messi (2026, May). [[La Prensa (Honduras)|La Prensa]]. 
https://www.laprensa.hn/deportes/seleccion-honduras-convocatoria-jugadores-amistoso-argentina-francisco-molina-previo-mundial-NK30812285</ref>

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Carlos Pineda (footballer)|Carlos Pineda]]|age={{bda|df=y|1997|9|23}}|caps=25|goals=0|club=[[Sporting F.C.|Sporting San José]]|clubnat=CRC|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 16 November 2025}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Romell Quioto]] <sup>RET</sup>|age={{bda|df=y|1991|8|9}}|caps=82|goals=19|club=[[Al-Ula FC|Al-Ula]]|clubnat=KSA|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 16 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[José Pinto (Honduran footballer)|José Pinto]]|age={{bda|df=y|1997|9|27}}|caps=20|goals=3|club=[[Sporting F.C.|Sporting San José]]|clubnat=CRC|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 16 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Justin Arboleda|Yustin Arboleda]]|age={{bda|df=y|1991|9|18}}|caps=14|goals=0|club=[[C.D. Olimpia|Olimpia]]|clubnat=HON|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 16 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Marlon Ramírez]]|age={{bda|df=y|1994|4|17}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Olancho FC|Olancho]]|clubnat=HON|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 16 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Anthony Lozano]]|other=[[Captain (association football)|captain]]|age={{bda|df=y|1993|4|25}}|caps=64|goals=16|club=''[[Free agent|Unattached]]''|clubnat=|latest=v. {{fb|HAI}}, 13 October 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Dixon Ramírez]]|age={{bda|df=y|2001|4|15}}|caps=4|goals=1|club=[[Real C.D. España|Real España]]|clubnat=HON|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 9 October 2025 <sup>INJ</sup>}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Nayrobi Vargas]]|age={{bda|df=y|2006|5|20}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[C.D. Marathón|Marathón]]|clubnat=HON|latest=v. {{fb|CRC}}, 9 October 2025 <sup>EXC</sup>}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> Player withdrew due to injury<br>
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew for personal reasons<br>
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad<br>
<sup>EXC</sup> Excluded from squad<br>
<sup>SUS</sup> Suspended<br>
<sup>RET</sup> Player retired from the national team<br>
{{nat fs end}}

==Records==
{{updated|19 November 2025}}<ref>{{cite web |last1=Passo Alpuin |first1=Luis Fernando |title=Honduras - Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/hond-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=30 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230330193345/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/hond-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Honduras.''

===Most appearances===
[[File:Maynor Figueroa 2017.jpg|thumb|200px|Maynor Figueroa is Honduras's most capped player with 181 appearances.]]

{| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align: center;"
|-
!width=30px|Rank
!width=150px|Player
!width=50px|Caps
!width=50px|Goals
!width=100px|Career
|-
| 1 ||align=left|[[Maynor Figueroa]] || 181 || 5 || 2003–2022
|-
| 2 ||align=left|[[Amado Guevara]] || 138 || 27 || 1994–2010
|-
| 3 ||align=left|[[Noel Valladares]]|| 135 || 0 || 2000–2016
|-
| 4 ||align=left|[[Boniek García]]|| 134 || 3 || 2005–2021
|-
| 5 ||align=left|[[Emilio Izaguirre]] || 111 || 5 || 2007–2020
|-
| 6 ||align=left|[[Carlos Pavón]] || 101 || 57 || 1993–2010
|-
| 7 ||align=left|[[Wilson Palacios]] || 97 || 5 || 2003–2014
|-
| 8 ||align=left|[[Danilo Turcios]] || 87 || 7 || 1999–2010
|-

|-
|align=left|[[Milton Núñez]] || 86 || 33 || 1994–2008
|-
|}

===CONCACAF Nations League===
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
|-
! colspan=21|[[CONCACAF Nations League]] record
|-
! colspan=10|League phase / Quarter-finals
! rowspan=8|
! colspan=9|Finals
|-
!Season
!Division
!Group
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Drawn}}
!{{Tooltip|L|Lost}}
!{{Tooltip|GF|Goals for}}
!{{Tooltip|GA|Goals against}}
!{{Tooltip|P/R|Promotion/relegation at end of season}}
!Year
!Result
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Drawn}}
!{{Tooltip|L|Lost}}
!{{Tooltip|GF|Goals for}}
!{{Tooltip|GA|Goals against}}

{| class="wikitable collapsible collapsed" style="text-align: center;font-size:90%;"
|-
!colspan=2|CONCACAF Nations League history
|-
!First match
|{{fb|Trinidad and Tobago}} 0–2 {{fb-rt|Honduras|1949}}<br> (10 October 2019; [[Port of Spain]], Trinidad and Tobago)
|-
!Biggest win
|{{fb|Honduras|1949}} 4–0 {{fb-rt|Trinidad and Tobago}}<br>(17 October 2019; [[San Pedro Sula]], Honduras)<br/>{{fb|Honduras}} 4–0 {{fb-rt|Grenada}}<br>(12 September 2023; [[Tegucigalpa]], Honduras)<br/>{{fb|Honduras}} 4–0 {{fb-rt|Cuba}}<br>(15 October 2023; [[Tegucigalpa]], Honduras)<br/>{{fb|Honduras}} 4–0 {{fb-rt|Trinidad and Tobago}}<br>(6 September 2024; [[Tegucigalpa]], Honduras)
|-
!Biggest defeat
|{{fb|Mexico}} 4–0 {{fb-rt|Honduras}}<br>(19 November 2024; [[Toluca]], Mexico)
|-
!Best result
|'''Third place''' ([[2021 CONCACAF Nations League Finals|2019–20]])
|-
!Worst result
|Seventh place ([[2022–23 CONCACAF Nations League|2022–23]])
|}

===Copa América===
{{main|Honduras at the Copa América}}

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;"
|-
!colspan=10|[[Copa América]] record
|-
!width=90|Year
!width=90|Round
!width=60|Position
!width=20|{{Abbr|Pld|Games played}}
!width=20|{{Abbr|W|Won}}
!width=20|{{Abbr|D|Drawn}}
!width=20|{{Abbr|L|Lost}}
!width=20|{{Abbr|GF|Goals for}}
!width=20|{{Abbr|GA|Goals against}}
!width=60|Squad
|- style="background:#c96;"
|{{flagicon|COL}} [[2001 Copa América|2001]]||'''Third place'''||'''3rd'''||'''6'''||'''3'''||'''1'''||'''2'''||'''7'''||'''5'''
|'''[[2001 Copa América squads#Honduras|Squad]]'''

|-
|{{flagicon|USA}} [[2024 Copa América|2024]]
|-
!Total!!Third place!!1/3!!6!!3!!1!!2!!7!!5!!—
|}
{| class="wikitable collapsible collapsed" style="text-align:center; font-size:90%;"
|-
!colspan=2|Copa América history
|-
!First match
|{{fb-rt|HON|1949}} 0–1 {{fb|CRC}}<br>(13 July 2001; [[Medellín]], Colombia)
|-
!Biggest win
|{{fb-rt|HON|1949}} 2–0 {{fb|BOL}}<br>(16 July 2001; [[Medellín]], Colombia)<br>{{fb-rt|HON|1949}} 2–0 {{fb|BRA}}<br>(23 July 2001; [[Manizales]], Colombia)
|-
!Biggest defeat
|{{fb|COL}} 2–0 {{fb-rt|HON|1949}}<br>(26 July 2001; [[Manizales]], Colombia)
|-
!Best result 
|'''Third place''' ([[2001 Copa América|2001]])
|-
!Worst result
|—
|}

{{notelist}}

==Honours==
===Continental===
* '''[[CONCACAF Championship]] / [[CONCACAF Gold Cup|Gold Cup]]'''
** [[File:CONCACAF - Gold Cup.svg|22px]] '''Champions (1)''': [[1981 CONCACAF Championship|1981]]
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[1985 CONCACAF Championship|1985]], [[1991 CONCACAF Gold Cup|1991]]
** {{bronze3}} Third place (1): [[1967 CONCACAF Championship|1967]]
* '''[[CONCACAF Nations League]]'''
** {{bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2021 CONCACAF Nations League Finals|2019–20]]
* '''[[Copa América|CONMEBOL Copa América]]'''
** {{bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2001 Copa América|2001]]

===Subregional===
* '''[[CCCF Championship]]'''{{sup|1}}
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1953 CCCF Championship|1953]]
** {{bronze3}} Third place (4): [[1955 CCCF Championship|1955]], [[1957 CCCF Championship|1957]], [[1960 CCCF Championship|1960]], [[1961 CCCF Championship|1961]]
* '''[[Copa Centroamericana|Copa de Naciones UNCAF / Copa Centroamericana]]'''
** {{gold1}} '''Champions (4)''': [[1993 UNCAF Nations Cup|1993]], [[1995 UNCAF Nations Cup|1995]], [[2011 Copa Centroamericana|2011]], [[2017 Copa Centroamericana|2017]]
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (3): [[1991 UNCAF Nations Cup|1991]], [[2005 UNCAF Nations Cup|2005]], [[2013 Copa Centroamericana|2013]]
** {{bronze3}} Third place (2): [[1999 UNCAF Nations Cup|1999]], [[2009 UNCAF Nations Cup|2009]]
* '''[[Football at the Central American and Caribbean Games|Central American and Caribbean Games]]'''
** {{silver2}} Silver medal (1): [[Football at the 1986 Central American and Caribbean Games|1986]]
** {{bronze3}} Bronze medal (1): [[Football at the 1930 Central American and Caribbean Games|1930]]

===Friendly===
* '''[[Lunar New Year Cup]] (1)''': [[2002 Lunar New Year Cup|2002]]
* '''Copa Independencia (1)''': 2010

===Summary===
Only official honours are included, according to ''FIFA statutes'' (competitions organized/recognized by [[FIFA]] or an affiliated confederation).

!Competition!!{{gold1}}!!{{silver2}}!!{{bronze3}}!!Total 
|-
|align=left|[[CONCACAF Championship]] / [[CONCACAF Gold Cup|Gold Cup]]
|1||2||1||4
|-

|0||0||1||1
|-

|0||0||1||1
|-

|0||1||4||5
|-

|}

;Notes
# Official subregional competition organized by [[Confederación Centroamericana y del Caribe de Fútbol|CCCF]], direct predecessor confederation of CONCACAF and the former governing body of football in Central America and Caribbean (1938–1961).
